turns.content: Can I take ibuprofen and paracetamol together?

Yes — ibuprofen and paracetamol can be taken together and this combination is commonly used for better pain relief than either alone. They work through different mechanisms: paracetamol acts centrally, ibuprofen is an anti-inflammatory. Standard adult doses: paracetamol up to 1g every 4-6 hours (max 4g/day), ibuprofen 400mg every 6-8 hours with food. Main cautions: ibuprofen can irritate the stomach and should not be taken on an empty stomach or by people with kidney issues or ulcer history.
What about taking them with blood pressure medication?

This is where it becomes more important to check with your pharmacist or doctor. Ibuprofen and NSAIDs generally can reduce the effectiveness of certain blood pressure medications — particularly ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics — and can cause sodium and water retention that raises blood pressure. If you are on blood pressure medication, paracetamol is generally the safer choice for pain relief. Your pharmacist can give you a precise answer in under two minutes — that is the right resource for medication interactions.
I'm on amlodipine. Is that one of the affected ones?

Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker, which has less interaction with NSAIDs compared to ACE inhibitors or diuretics. The concern is lower than with some other antihypertensives. That said, ibuprofen can still modestly elevate blood pressure in some individuals regardless of which medication they are on. Paracetamol remains the safer choice for regular use. For occasional use, ibuprofen with amlodipine is generally considered acceptable by most guidelines at the lowest effective dose. A quick call to your pharmacist with your full medication list is worth it — I am not your prescribing doctor.
